perturbation.original_text: LMG shall provide training to TAG with respect to the use of the LMG Tools, Other Third Party Software and Documentation as reasonably requested by TAG.
perturbation.changed_text: LMG may provide training to TAG with respect to the use of the LMG Tools, Other Third Party Software and Documentation if LMG deems it necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that LMG 'shall provide' training upon request, making it an obligation. The modified text makes training discretionary ('may provide' if LMG 'deems it necessary'), which could contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance, as it allows LMG to arbitrarily deny training necessary for TAG to effectively use the licensed tools and fulfill its obligations. The duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations, and denying necessary training could be seen as a breach of this duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes LMG's obligation to provide training from 'shall' to 'may' based on LMG's discretion. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The original text imposed a clear duty which the changed text weakens, potentially allowing LMG to deny necessary training, violating the obligation of good faith. This falls under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because 'shall' and 'may' have distinct legal meanings, and the change undermines a legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: LMG shall exercise commercially reasonable efforts to maintain the employment of the LMG employees identified in Exhibit B ("Retained Employees") for the estimated retention period specified in Exhibit B or such other period as TAG may reasonably request ("Retention Period"). LMG shall cause them to devote the same amount of time and attention to the performance of the services under this Agreement as they presently devote to such services. In all events, LMG shall retain a sufficient number of qualified personnel to perform the LMG Services.
perturbation.changed_text: LMG will attempt to maintain the employment of the LMG employees identified in Exhibit B ("Retained Employees") for the estimated retention period specified in Exhibit B or such other period as TAG may request ("Retention Period"). LMG shall encourage them to devote time and attention to the performance of the services under this Agreement. LMG shall endeavor to retain qualified personnel to perform the LMG Services.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause imposes a 'shall' obligation on LMG to retain employees and ensure their devotion to services. The modified version weakens this commitment using terms like 'attempt,' 'encourage,' and 'endeavor,' changing a clear obligation into a weaker effort.  This creates uncertainty about the level of commitment required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Mitigate Damages
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to retain employees, the modified clause could potentially contradict the principle of mitigation of damages in contract law.  If LMG's failure to adequately retain qualified personnel leads to deficient service and causes damages to TAG, TAG might argue that LMG failed to take reasonable steps to mitigate those damages by ensuring sufficient staffing.  The original stronger language supports the argument that LMG had a clear duty to maintain staffing levels, whereas the modified language weakens that argument.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: mitigation of damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you mitigation of damages The mitigation of damages doctrine, also known as the doctrine of avoidable consequences, prevents an injured party from recovering damages that could have been avoided through reasonable efforts. The duty to mitigate damages is most traditionally employed in the areas of tort and contract law. In a breach of contract case, upon receiving notice that one party to a contract does not intend to perform, the other party is required to mitigate damages, meaning that it must take reasonable efforts to avoid further losses from the breach. The duty to mitigate damages was famously illustrated in contract law in Luten Bridge Co. vs. Rockingham County where Rockingham County hired Luten Bridge Co. to construct a bridge. Thereafter, the County voted not to continue with the construction of the bridge and informed Luten to cease construction of the bridge. However, Luten continued construction, then filed suit seeking damages for the counties breach of contract. The court held that Luten had a duty to stop construction and that a contractor cannot continue to work on a project and increase the damages stemming from the breach of contract. Mitigation of damages has also been invoked in the field of property law. For example, if a tenant abandons their lease , a landlord has duty to mitigate damages caused by the breaching tenant. In this case, the mitigation of damages doctrine imposes a duty on the landlord to try to find a new tenant and re-rent their property. [Last reviewed in June of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE contracts wex definitions type Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation on LMG to retain employees, potentially contradicting the principle of mitigation of damages. The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a firm obligation, while the changed text uses weaker terms like 'attempt,' 'encourage,' and 'endeavor.' This shift from a clear duty to a weaker effort could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable steps to mitigate damages if LMG's failure to retain qualified personnel leads to deficient service and causes damages to TAG. The scraped law snippet defines mitigation of damages as preventing an injured party from recovering damages that could have been avoided through reasonable efforts. By weakening the obligation to retain employees, LMG arguably fails to take these reasonable efforts, potentially leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party materially defaults in the performance of its duties or obligations set forth in this Agreement, and such default is not cured within thirty (30) days after written notice is given to the defaulting party specifying the default, then the party not in default may, by giving written notice thereof to the defaulting party, terminate the Agreement as of a date specified in such notice of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party believes a default has occurred in the performance of its duties or obligations under this agreement, and such belief is communicated to the defaulting party, then the party communicating such belief may terminate the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a 'material default' that is not 'cured within thirty (30) days'. The change eliminates the materiality requirement, the 30-day cure period, and the need for formal written notice specifying the default. It allows termination based on a subjective 'belief' of default, making it much easier and faster to terminate the agreement, which would be a violation of the business laws of California. This means if it is based on the modified text, the agreement can be terminated almost immediately if they believe something happened.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the conditions for termination, which can restrict the trade and profession of one of the parties. The elimination of a material default and cure period, and the introduction of a subjective belief standard, make the termination clause overly broad and potentially unenforceable under California law. Section 16600 prohibits contracts that restrain a party from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business, and this change could be seen as an unreasonable restraint due to the ease with which the contract can be terminated, thus affecting the future possibilities of LMG, the CA based company.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a). The original text necessitates a 'material default' and a 30-day cure period before termination, whereas the modified text allows termination based on a subjective 'belief' of default without any cure period. This weakens the protection against arbitrary termination, potentially restraining a party from engaging in their lawful trade or business, which is prohibited under BPC 16600(a): 'Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.'
